Pre-Read for Class 1

The Heterogeneity of Profitability Among U.S. Listed Firms, 2015-2025

Written by Jacopo Perego for the MBA course Economics of Strategic Behavior at Columbia Business School.

Introduction

Why are some firms persistently more successful than others, even when they appear to operate in similar markets and face similar customers, suppliers, technologies, and competitors? This is one of the central questions in strategy and industrial organization.

The graphs on this page illustrate the average profitability over the last decade of 918 U.S. listed firms. As one would expect, profitability varies enormously across companies. More surprisingly, only a modest share of this variation is explained by the industry in which a firm competes. In our sample, industry membership explains about 18.1% of the variation in profitability when profitability is measured by Operating ROA, and only about 4.3% when it is measured by Operating Margin.

This creates an important puzzle. If firms in the same industry face broadly similar economic conditions, why do they often perform so differently? Why are some firms able to earn much higher returns than their peers? These are among the key questions we try to answer in this course. A great deal of the action is not simply across industries, but within them. That is where strategy becomes especially important: understanding why some firms, facing similar broad industry conditions, build stronger positions, make better choices, and achieve persistently superior performance.

This page documents this empirical fact and allows you to explore it directly. The All Firms tab shows the overall distribution of profitability across firms, using each firm's average profitability across the fiscal years available in the data. The By Industry tab breaks these same firm-level averages into selected industry groups. It reports each industry's median profitability and shows how much of the total variation comes from differences across industries versus differences among firms within the same industry.

There are two important caveats to keep in mind.

First, profitability is difficult to measure, especially using public data at this scale. Ideally, we would like to measure economic profit: for example, whether a firm earns returns above its cost of capital. But that is difficult to compute cleanly for hundreds of companies. We therefore use two imperfect but useful measures: Operating ROA and Operating Margin. Operating ROA asks how much operating profit a firm generates per dollar of assets. Operating Margin asks how much operating profit the firm keeps from each dollar of sales.

Second, defining the industry in which a firm operates is more complicated than it may seem. Standard industry classifications have important limitations. Here, firms are classified using an LLM based on detailed company descriptions extracted from Wikipedia. Firms that do not fit cleanly into one of the predefined categories are assigned to a residual category.

Variance Decomposition

How much of the cross-firm heterogeneity in profitability is explained by the industry in which firms operate? The between-industry share captures the part of this heterogeneity that can be explained by industry membership. The within-industry share captures the remaining variation: differences among firms operating in the same industry. These two shares add up to 100%. Technically, the between-industry share is the R-squared from a regression of firm profitability on industry fixed effects. This calculation uses each firm's primary industry assignment and excludes the same margin outliers that are hidden from the chart. A small between-industry share means that industry explains only a limited part of profitability. Most of the variation is within industries, among direct peers. That is the part strategy tries to explain.

Operating ROA

Industry Distributions

Data Notes

We start from 1,000 firms in the iShares Russell 1000 ETF holdings file, as of May 27, 2026. We match firms to SEC Company Facts, collapse duplicate share classes to one economic entity, and apply minimum-history filters to build the source panel. In this run, 1 ticker did not match a SEC CIK, 9 duplicate share-class/security rows were removed, and 47 firms had too little usable history for both metrics. The source panel has 918 firms and 8,874 firm-year observations. The charts use the selected year-window rule: each dot is one firm's average for the selected metric over the selected recent fiscal-year window, shown only when the firm has data for every year in that window; averages above 100% or below -100% are hidden as outliers.
